# Contributing to WkMCP

Thanks for your interest! The fastest way in is to read `README.md`, then
`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (the contributor doc: IPC, cache, parser, and how to add an
MCP tool or a daemon verb).

## Build & test

```powershell
dotnet build src/WkDaemon -c Release   # daemon first (deploys native binaries)
dotnet build src/WkMcp -c Release
dotnet test  src/WkMcp.Tests -c Release # includes an MCP E2E smoke test
./build-mcpb.ps1 -Configuration Release        # builds dist/wkmcp.mcpb
```

Build/test in **`-c Release`**: a running server instance locks the Debug DLL.

Reference toolchain: .NET 8; `ModelContextProtocol` 1.4.0 (+ AspNetCore);
`WolvenKit.Modkit` 8.18.0; `YamlDotNet` 16.3.0; `cp77tools` = WolvenKit.CLI 8.18.0
(fallback).

## The anti-drift contract (do not break it)

`ConsistencyTests` (`src/WkMcp.Tests/ConsistencyTests.cs`) keeps the docs
and code in sync. Before opening a PR, ensure `dotnet test` is green — in
particular:

- Every tool name cited in a prompt or in the `wkmcp://reference` resource
  must exist in the assembly (checked by reflection).
- `README.md` must mention every prompt name and every resource URI stem, and
  must contain the literal phrases `123 tools`, `8 prompts`, `4 resources`
  (the counts are read from the code by reflection). If you add or remove a
  tool, prompt, or resource, update the README counts/tables so these stay true.
- Every `[McpServerTool]` must declare its `ReadOnly`/`Destructive`/`Idempotent`
  hints explicitly.

The `wkmcp://reference` cheat sheet is **generated by reflection** from the
registered tools — never reintroduce a hardcoded tool count there.

## Adding an MCP tool / a daemon verb

See `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` §6 (add an MCP tool) and §7 (add a daemon verb). In
short: a tool is a static method in a `[McpServerToolType]` class
(`WolvenKitTools.cs` / `ModdingTools.cs` / `LiveTools.cs`); registration is
automatic via reflection. Add the annotation hints, a clear `[Description]`, and
a test where relevant.

## Pull requests

- Keep one concern per PR.
- Run `dotnet test` before pushing.
- Update `CHANGELOG.md` under the relevant version heading.
- If your change is user-facing, keep `README.md` and `docs/` consistent (the
  `ConsistencyTests` above will fail otherwise).

## Known pitfalls (hard-won knowledge)

- The daemon needs `kraken.dll` and `DirectXTexNet.dll` — deployed by the
  `DeployNativeWindows` target of the csproj + the `AssemblyLoadContext`
  resolver.
- `cp77tools` sometimes returns a non-zero exit code on success (e.g. `oodle`);
  result formatting relies on the `: Success` / `: Error` log markers, not the
  exit code.
- `WolvenKit.CLI` keeps its host `internal` → the daemon rebuilds the DI
  container from the public types of the libraries.
- The **first call** of a session waits for the daemon warm-up (~7 s).
- `resolve_hash` and the `tweakdb_*` tools work **only via the daemon** (no
  `cp77tools` subprocess fallback).
- `export_entity` remains experimental: `IModTools.ExportEntity` refuses in
  headless mode on NPC entities (the GUI does an undocumented setup).
- `build-mcpb.ps1` must keep its **UTF-8 BOM** (Windows PowerShell 5.1 does not
  parse accented UTF-8 without it; CI uses `pwsh`, which doesn't care).

## Real-game validation (optional, manual)

`validate-windows.py` drives the tools against a real Cyberpunk 2077 install
(long). The `live_*` tools require the game running with the CETBridge mod
(`test-live-bridge.py`). These are not run in CI.
